title: Ceraia dentata
taxonomy: Ceraia dentata is classified within the family Hymenopogonae, a group of flowering plants in the orchid family (Orchidaceae). It is further classified into the subfamily Orchidoideae. This species is also known to belong to the genus Ceraia, which is a part of the larger family of orchids. The classification of Ceraia dentata is further supported by its unique characteristics, such as its distinct flower structure and growth habits. By studying its classification, researchers can gain a better understanding of the evolutionary history and relationships between different orchid species.

intro: Ceraia dentata, also known as Strychnos nux-vomica, is a plant species belonging to the Loganiaceae family, native to Southeast Asia and commonly found in the tropical forests of India, Malaysia, and Indonesia. The bark and seeds of Ceraia dentata are used in traditional medicine due to their potential health benefits, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, making it useful in treating various ailments such as fever, diarrhea, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Ceraia dentata include alkaloids, glycosides, and terpenoids, with the most notable being strychnine, which has been traditionally used as a medicinal and psychoactive agent. The most common herbal preparations made with Ceraia dentata include decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which are used in traditional medicine to treat a range of health conditions, although its use is strictly regulated due to the presence of toxic compounds like strychnine.
